title: JEE Main 2026
meta_description: JEE Main 2026 — NTA engineering entrance exam for NITs, IIITs, GFTIs. Exam pattern, syllabus, cutoff, and JEE Advanced eligibility.

hero.one_liner: India's largest engineering entrance exam — gateway to 31 NITs, 26 IIITs, and 33 GFTIs, plus qualifying for JEE Advanced (IIT admission).

what_is_it: JEE Main (Joint Entrance Examination Main) is India's largest engineering entrance exam, conducted by NTA (National Testing Agency) twice annually for admission to undergraduate B.Tech/B.E. programs at 31 NITs (National Institutes of Technology), 26 IIITs (Indian Institutes of Information Technology), 33 GFTIs (Government Funded Technical Institutions), and thousands of private engineering colleges. It also serves as the qualifying gateway — only the top 2.5 lakh JEE Main qualifiers (category-wise) can appear for JEE Advanced, which is the sole pathway to 23 IITs. With 12+ lakh applicants competing, JEE Main is the most competitive engineering exam globally by number of candidates.

JEE Main is conducted in two sessions: Session 1 (January 21-30, 2026) and Session 2 (April 1-10, 2026). Candidates can appear in one or both sessions — the better score is considered for ranking. The two-session format ensures fairness and allows candidates a second chance. Paper 1 (B.E./B.Tech) tests Physics, Chemistry, and Mathematics with 90 questions in 3 hours. Each subject has 30 questions: 20 MCQs (4 marks each, -1 for wrong) and 10 Numerical Answer Type (4 marks each, no negative, attempt any 5 out of 10). Total attempt: 75 questions, 300 marks.

NIT placements are excellent — NIT Trichy, Warangal, Surathkal, and Allahabad are among India's best engineering colleges, with average packages of ₹8-15 LPA. Many students clearing JEE Main with 99+ percentile also appear for JEE Advanced to compete for IIT seats. However, even without JEE Advanced, top NIT seats guarantee strong career outcomes. The exam tests conceptual understanding rather than pure memorization — Physics requires problem-solving ability, Chemistry requires precise knowledge of reactions and mechanisms, Mathematics requires logical reasoning and calculations.

JEE Main allows 6 total attempts across 3 years (two sessions per year). Many successful candidates take 2-3 attempts — using Session 1 as a trial run and Session 2 as the main attempt, or spreading attempts across multiple years for improvement. The percentile-based ranking ensures fairness across sessions through normalization algorithms.

key_details.label: Education Requirement
key_details.value: 12th pass with Physics, Chemistry, Mathematics. Appearing students eligible.

key_details.label: Age Limit
key_details.value: No upper age limit (removed in 2019). Must have passed 12th in 2024, 2025, or 2026.

key_details.label: Exam Attempts
key_details.value: 6 sessions over 3 consecutive years (2 per year)

key_details.label: Exam Mode
key_details.value: Computer Based Test (CBT) online at designated centers

key_details.label: JEE Advanced Eligibility
key_details.value: Top 2.5 lakh JEE Main qualifiers (category-wise) can appear for JEE Advanced

exam_pattern.description: Each subject divided into Section A (20 MCQs, compulsory) and Section B (10 Numerical, attempt any 5). Total attempt: 75 questions from 90. NTA conducts in two shifts daily (9-12 AM and 3-6 PM).
exam_pattern.negative_marking: MCQs: -1 for wrong answer. Numerical: No negative marking. +4 for all correct answers.

rows: Very High — conceptual depth + advanced application
NIT Trichy, IIIT Hyderabad, NIT Warangal

extra_sections.heading: Preparation Strategy by Subject
content:
Physics — Conceptual Application

Physics requires understanding of principles, not memorization.

Key chapters (60% weightage): Mechanics (kinematics, dynamics, work-energy, rotational motion), Electrostatics (Coulomb's law, electric field, potential), Current Electricity (Ohm's law, circuits), Magnetism, Optics (ray optics, wave optics), and Modern Physics (photoelectric effect, atomic models).
Study HC Verma (theoretical understanding) + solve problems from Previous Year JEE papers. Practice numericals daily — Physics is 50% calculation.

Chemistry — Memory + Application Balance

Chemistry has three components: (1) Inorganic (30% — reactions, oxidation states, periodic trends), (2) Organic (35% — mechanisms, reactions, nomenclature), (3) Physical (35% — thermodynamics, kinetics, equilibrium). Inorganic Chemistry is heavily memorization-based.
MS Chouhan's Organic Chemistry book is the standard. Physical Chemistry requires problem-solving.
Chemistry is the 'easiest' to score high in JEE Main — many toppers score 95+ in Chemistry with less effort than Physics.

Mathematics — Consistency & Accuracy

Math is 40% easy, 40% moderate, 20% hard. Easy topics (Trigonometry, Coordinate Geometry, Algebra basics) form 50% of paper — scoring 40+ in math requires mastering these.
Hard topics (Calculus, 3D Geometry, Probability) form 20% — don't spend excessive time here. RD Sharma for theory, Cengage/Arihant for advanced problems.
Mathematics needs daily practice — aim for accuracy over speed.

preparation_tips: NCERT textbooks (11th + 12th) are the foundation — 60-70% of JEE Main questions are NCERT-level or slight variations. Read NCERT chapters 2-3 times, highlighting important formulas and concepts. Don't skip NCERT and jump directly to advanced books. Most toppers spend month 1-2 completing NCERT thoroughly before touching coaching material.

Chemistry is the highest-scoring subject in JEE Main — especially Inorganic Chemistry which is factual (reactions, trends, properties) and Organic Chemistry which is mechanism-based. Physics and Math are harder. Strategic approach: Aim for 90+ in Chemistry, 75-85 in Physics, 75-85 in Math = 240-260+ score (99+ percentile). Chemistry is your safety net for high percentile.

Numerical Answer Type (NAT) questions have zero negative marking — attempt ALL of them even if uncertain. Even rough approximations give 25% chance of correctness. On the other hand, MCQs have -1 penalty — never guess on MCQs. Typical strategy: Solve all NAT questions (loose marking), solve certain MCQs only (strict selection), skip uncertain MCQs completely.

Use both JEE Main sessions strategically. Session 1 is for 'practice' — don't stress about score. Session 2 (April) is the main attempt where you apply learnings from Session 1. This removes pressure and gives realistic scoring. Many candidates score 10-20 percentile points higher in Session 2 due to experience and focused preparation for weak areas identified in Session 1.

Take minimum 10-15 full mock tests from month 4 onwards. Timing matters — practice solving within 3-hour time limit. Analyze mock test performance: Which topics are weak? Why did I lose marks? Silly mistakes or concept gaps? Keep a chapter-wise performance log. Retake the same 3-4 mock papers every 2 weeks to track improvement.

faqs.question: Is 12th board percentage important for JEE Main ranking?
faqs.answer: For JEE Main ranking: Only JEE Main score matters — board percentage is irrelevant. For NIT/IIIT admission: You need 75% aggregate in 12th (65% for SC/ST) as eligibility, but this doesn't impact ranking. Your JEE Main percentile alone determines merit. However, some state engineering colleges have their own cutoffs based on board score. Bottom line: Focus 100% on JEE Main score, not board percentage.

faqs.question: Can I get into an IIT through JEE Main alone?
faqs.answer: No. JEE Main qualifies you for JEE Advanced. Only through JEE Advanced (separate exam) can you get into IITs. However, JEE Main directly gets you into NITs, IIITs, and GFTIs — which are excellent institutions. NIT Trichy or IIIT Hyderabad are comparable to lower IITs in placements and opportunities. If your goal is specifically IIT, prepare for both JEE Main and Advanced. If you're satisfied with NIT/IIIT, focus on JEE Main only.

faqs.question: Should I take coaching for JEE Main?
faqs.answer: Coaching is helpful but not mandatory. Many self-study toppers exist, especially with free online resources (Physics Wallah, Unacademy free classes, YouTube tutorials). Coaching benefits: structured curriculum, doubt solving, regular mocks. Cost-benefit: Classroom coaching (₹1-3 lakh) is expensive. Online subscriptions (₹10-30K) offer better value. Ideal: Use free YouTube for basics, then join low-cost online program for difficult topics. Don't depend entirely on coaching — self-study and consistent practice are non-negotiable.

faqs.question: What percentile is needed for top NITs in computer science?
faqs.answer: Top NIT CSE (Trichy, Warangal, Surathkal): 99.5+ percentile (General). NIT Rourkee/Delhi CSE: 98.5-99.5. Mid-tier NIT CSE: 96-98.5. Non-CSE branches at top NITs: 95-97. OBC cutoffs are 1-2 percentile lower. SC/ST cutoffs are 5-10 percentile lower. These are approximate — actual cutoffs vary based on total applicants. If you're targeting NIT CSE, aim for 99+ to be safe. Scoring 98 doesn't guarantee CSE at top NIT but gets you CSE at mid-tier or non-CSE at top NIT.

faqs.question: I failed to get into IIT through JEE Advanced. Is NIT still good?
faqs.answer: Yes, absolutely. NIT placements rival lower IITs. Top NIT graduates work at Microsoft, Google, Goldman Sachs, Tesla, with packages ₹10-25 LPA. Career depends on your effort and skills, not college alone. Many successful startup founders and researchers are NIT alumni. NIT Trichy has better CSE placements than some IITs. The difference between top IIT and top NIT is primarily in brand value and ease of internship opportunities, not actual skill development. Many NIT graduates earn more than IIT graduates due to personal effort.

syllabus.subject: Physics
syllabus.topics: Mechanics (kinematics, laws of motion, work energy power, rotational motion), thermodynamics, waves & oscillations, electrostatics, current electricity, magnetism, electromagnetic induction, optics (ray + wave), modern physics, semiconductor devices

syllabus.subject: Chemistry
syllabus.topics: Physical chemistry (mole concept, thermodynamics, equilibrium, electrochemistry, kinetics), organic chemistry (GOC, hydrocarbons, alcohols, aldehydes, amines, polymers), inorganic chemistry (periodic table, p-block, d-block, coordination compounds, metallurgy)

syllabus.subject: Mathematics
syllabus.topics: Algebra (complex numbers, quadratic equations, matrices, permutations), calculus (limits, differentiation, integration, differential equations), coordinate geometry (straight lines, circles, conics), trigonometry, vectors & 3D geometry, probability & statistics

study_plan.tasks: Complete NCERT textbooks thoroughly — all examples and exercises. Start H.C. Verma physics alongside school. Focus on understanding concepts, not memorizing. Join a coaching institute or online program (Physics Wallah, Unacademy).

Complete 12th syllabus in school + coaching. Start solving previous year JEE questions chapter-wise. Organic chemistry — practice reaction mechanisms daily. Maths — solve R.D. Sharma objective problems.
Complete all previous year papers (2015-2025). Take full mock tests weekly. Focus on NCERT-based questions — 30-40% of JEE Main comes directly from NCERT concepts. Revise formula sheets daily.

Daily mock test + analysis. Target: 250+ for top NITs, 180+ for decent NITs. Focus on scoring topics — thermodynamics, modern physics, coordinate geometry, organic named reactions. No new topics — only revision and practice.

quiz.question: A body is thrown vertically upward with velocity u. The maximum height reached is:
The hybridization of carbon in benzene is:
The derivative of sin²x with respect to x is:
In the reaction 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, 4 grams of H₂ reacts with how many grams of O₂?
